content.text: Hallucination guardrails are post-generation checks that intercept an LLM's output before it reaches the user or downstream system, verifying that the text is factually grounded, internally consistent, and supported by available evidence. Unlike safety filters that detect harmful content, hallucination guardrails address a subtler failure mode: the model producing fluent, confident-sounding text that is simply wrong -- fabricated citations, incorrect numerical values, invented API signatures, or plausible-but-false causal claims.

The core challenge is that language models have no intrinsic notion of truth. They predict the next most-probable token given context, which means a model can generate false statements with exactly the same fluency and apparent confidence as true ones. Hallucination guardrails impose an external verification step, using techniques ranging from retrieval-augmented grounding checks to natural language inference (NLI) classifiers to self-consistency sampling -- each catching a different failure mode and together reducing the rate at which fabricated content escapes to users.

In conversational assistants, a hallucinated fact is an inconvenience. In agentic systems, it becomes an action. An agent that invents a tool argument, fabricates a customer record value, or confidently cites a non-existent regulation can trigger irreversible downstream effects -- a wrong API call, a misconfigured system, a compliance report filed with false data. The cost of a single hallucination scales with the degree of real-world autonomy granted to the agent.

Hallucination rates also vary with domain, prompt structure, and model version in ways that are not always predictable. A model that performs well on a benchmark may still hallucinate on long-tail queries, edge cases, or topics that are underrepresented in training data. Guardrails provide a runtime safety net that is independent of the model's internal training -- they remain effective even as the underlying model changes or is swapped.
Verified outputs contain claims traceable to source documents, reducing user-facing errors in knowledge-intensive applications.
Blocking hallucinated tool arguments before they execute prevents agents from taking actions based on fabricated data.
Systems that acknowledge uncertainty rather than confabulate earn higher long-term trust than those that always produce a confident answer.
In regulated domains (healthcare, finance, legal), documented verification steps demonstrate due diligence when AI-generated content influences decisions.
Guardrails operate on output text, making them portable across model versions, fine-tunes, and provider changes without retraining.
When verification fails, the system can abstain or prompt the user to consult authoritative sources rather than propagating a wrong answer.
NLI classifiers add 50-150ms; a secondary LLM verification call adds 500ms-2s. This is often unacceptable for real-time chat interfaces.
Grounding checks only work when source documents exist. For open-ended generation without a retrieval corpus, there is nothing to verify against.
NLI models can flag a true paraphrase as a contradiction if the wording diverges significantly from the source. Overly strict thresholds degrade usefulness.
A model can be consistently wrong. If the training data contains a systematic error, all samples will agree on the incorrect answer.
Self-consistency requires N independent samples (typically 5-20), multiplying inference cost proportionally -- prohibitive without careful budget management.
NLI models trained on general text may perform poorly on specialized domains (clinical notes, legal documents, code). Domain-adapted classifiers are required but add maintenance burden.

Hallucination guardrails combine several verification techniques, each targeting a different failure mode. They operate as a pipeline of gates: the output passes each gate or is intercepted for handling.

When the system uses RAG, every factual claim in the generated output is compared to the retrieved source chunks. The guardrail extracts atomic claims from the output and checks that each claim is entailed by at least one source chunk. A claim with no supporting source is flagged as ungrounded. This is the strongest form of hallucination check because it anchors verification to authoritative external documents.

Natural language inference (NLI) classifies the relationship between a premise (source text) and a hypothesis (generated claim) as entailment, neutral, or contradiction. A classifier like cross-encoder/nli-deberta-v3-base predicts three class probabilities. The entailment score for a claim
A claim is considered grounded if
exceeds a threshold (commonly 0.7). For multi-chunk sources, the maximum entailment score across all chunks is used:

For factual questions without a retrieval corpus, self-consistency generates N independent samples at non-zero temperature and computes agreement across answers. The consistency score for a candidate answer
is the fraction of samples that agree:
High agreement (e.g., 80% of samples return the same answer) increases confidence. Disagreement signals model uncertainty and triggers abstention or escalation. Note that this measures
of the model's internal distribution, not ground truth.

Some guardrail implementations prompt the model itself to express uncertainty using structured output. The model returns a confidence field alongside its answer. While model self-reported confidence is imperfectly calibrated, it provides a fast signal that can be combined with NLI scores. Well-calibrated models produce an estimated probability that is close to empirical accuracy -- but this calibration must be measured and not assumed.
The diagram below shows how a generated output flows through sequential verification gates, with each gate independently capable of routing to the hallucination handler.

attrs.alt: Hallucination guardrail pipeline showing grounding check, factual scorer, confidence gate, and verified output

content.text: RAG-based systems where the model must answer from retrieved documents -- grounding checks are cheap and highly effective.
Agentic systems where generated outputs become tool arguments -- a hallucinated parameter can cause irreversible actions.
High-stakes domains: medical, legal, financial, or safety-critical information where an incorrect fact can harm users.
Document summarization pipelines where accuracy to the source document is contractually or regulatorily required.
When not to apply (or apply selectively)
Creative writing or brainstorming tasks where factual accuracy is not the goal -- guardrails would incorrectly flag imaginative content.
Open-ended question-answering without a retrieval corpus -- NLI grounding has nothing to check against and self-consistency is expensive without clear benefit.
High-volume, low-stakes applications (e.g., auto-complete suggestions) where the cost of verification exceeds the cost of an occasional inaccuracy.

Define your verification mode: choose grounding check (RAG systems with source docs), NLI entailment, self-consistency, or a combination. Match the technique to your corpus availability and latency budget.

Extract atomic claims from the output: split the generated text into individual factual statements before verifying. Verifying paragraphs against sources is less precise than verifying individual claims.
Load a cross-encoder NLI model (e.g., cross-encoder/nli-deberta-v3-base from sentence-transformers). Run each claim against the retrieved source chunks and record entailment scores.
Set a calibrated threshold: start at 0.7 entailment probability and tune against a labeled evaluation set of grounded and hallucinated outputs for your domain.

Implement a hallucination handler with three resolution paths: (a) abstain and return 'I don't have enough information', (b) flag for human review, or (c) re-generate with a stricter prompt that instructs the model to only assert what is in the sources.
Log all hallucination events with the flagged claim, source chunks, NLI score, and resolution action for monitoring and threshold tuning.
Instrument a hallucination rate metric (flagged outputs / total outputs) segmented by query type. Monitor for drift as the underlying LLM or retrieval corpus changes.

# hallucination_checker.py
from dataclasses import dataclass
from sentence_transformers import CrossEncoder

# Load once at module level; model is ~180MB
_nli_model = CrossEncoder("cross-encoder/nli-deberta-v3-base")

# Label indices returned by cross-encoder NLI models trained on MNLI
_CONTRADICTION = 0
_ENTAILMENT = 2

ENTAILMENT_THRESHOLD = 0.70  # tune against your evaluation set


@dataclass
class ClaimVerification:
    claim: str
    best_source: str
    entailment_score: float
    is_grounded: bool


def extract_claims(text: str) -> list[str]:
    """Split output into atomic claims for individual verification.
    Production systems use an LLM for this step; we use sentence splitting here.
    """
    import re
    sentences = re.split(r"(?<=[.!?])\s+", text.strip())
    return [s.strip() for s in sentences if len(s.strip()) > 20]


def verify_claims(
    claims: list[str],
    source_chunks: list[str],
    threshold: float = ENTAILMENT_THRESHOLD,
) -> list[ClaimVerification]:
    """Check each claim against all source chunks; return per-claim results."""
    results = []
    for claim in claims:
        # Build (source, claim) pairs for the cross-encoder
        pairs = [(src, claim) for src in source_chunks]
        scores = _nli_model.predict(pairs, apply_softmax=True)
        # scores shape: (len(source_chunks), 3) -- contradiction, neutral, entailment
        entailment_scores = scores[:, _ENTAILMENT]
        best_idx = int(entailment_scores.argmax())
        best_score = float(entailment_scores[best_idx])
        results.append(
            ClaimVerification(
                claim=claim,
                best_source=source_chunks[best_idx],
                entailment_score=best_score,
                is_grounded=best_score >= threshold,
            )
        )
    return results


def is_output_grounded(
    output: str,
    source_chunks: list[str],
    threshold: float = ENTAILMENT_THRESHOLD,
) -> tuple[bool, list[ClaimVerification]]:
    """Return (all_grounded, per_claim_details)."""
    claims = extract_claims(output)
    if not claims:
        return True, []
    verifications = verify_claims(claims, source_chunks, threshold)
    all_grounded = all(v.is_grounded for v in verifications)
    return all_grounded, verifications


# Usage example
if __name__ == "__main__":
    source_docs = [
        "The Eiffel Tower was built between 1887 and 1889 and stands 330 metres tall.",
        "Gustave Eiffel designed the tower as the entrance arch for the 1889 World's Fair.",
    ]
    candidate = (
        "The Eiffel Tower was constructed in 1887 and reaches a height of 330 metres. "
        "It was designed by Gustave Eiffel for the 1900 World's Fair."
    )  # Note: last sentence contains a hallucination (1900, not 1889)

    grounded, details = is_output_grounded(candidate, source_docs)
    for d in details:
        status = "OK" if d.is_grounded else "HALLUCINATION"
        print(f"[{status}] score={d.entailment_score:.2f} | {d.claim[:60]}")

# self_consistency.py
import re
from collections import Counter
from anthropic import Anthropic

client = Anthropic()

N_SAMPLES = 7          # generate 7 independent answers
TEMPERATURE = 0.7      # non-zero for diversity
MIN_AGREEMENT = 0.6    # require 60% of samples to agree


def normalize_answer(text: str) -> str:
    """Strip whitespace, lowercase, and remove trailing punctuation."""
    text = text.strip().lower()
    text = re.sub(r"[.!?,;]+$", "", text)
    return text


def self_consistent_answer(
    question: str,
    system: str = "Answer the question concisely in one sentence.",
) -> dict:
    """Return {'answer': str, 'confidence': float, 'abstain': bool}."""
    responses = []
    for _ in range(N_SAMPLES):
        msg = client.messages.create(
            model="claude-opus-4-5",
            max_tokens=256,
            temperature=TEMPERATURE,
            system=system,
            messages=[{"role": "user", "content": question}],
        )
        responses.append(msg.content[0].text)

    normalized = [normalize_answer(r) for r in responses]
    counts = Counter(normalized)
    most_common_answer, most_common_count = counts.most_common(1)[0]
    confidence = most_common_count / N_SAMPLES

    return {
        "answer": most_common_answer,
        "confidence": confidence,
        "abstain": confidence < MIN_AGREEMENT,
        "all_responses": responses,
        "agreement_distribution": dict(counts),
    }


if __name__ == "__main__":
    result = self_consistent_answer(
        "What year did the Berlin Wall fall?"
    )
    if result["abstain"]:
        print(f"Low confidence ({result['confidence']:.0%}) -- abstaining.")
    else:
        print(f"Answer: {result['answer']} (confidence: {result['confidence']:.0%})")

A legal technology company deploys an AI assistant that helps paralegals summarize case law documents. The assistant retrieves relevant precedents via RAG and generates summaries that may cite holdings, dates, and judge names. Incorrect citations can mislead legal filings, so the team implements a three-layer hallucination guardrail:
Atomic claim extraction via an LLM prompt that segments the summary into individual factual claims (case names, dates, holdings, citations).

NLI entailment check against retrieved case document chunks using a fine-tuned legal NLI model trained on the CUAD dataset. Threshold tuned to 0.75 based on precision-recall analysis against 1,200 labeled claim-source pairs.
Citation format verification: a regex check confirms that every case citation in the output (e.g., '347 U.S. 483') matches a citation present in the retrieved source documents.

Ungrounded claims trigger a summary regeneration with the prompt: 'Only assert facts that are explicitly stated in the provided case excerpts. For any claim without clear source support, omit it and note the gap.' This regeneration path resolves 78% of hallucinations without requiring human review, and the remaining 22% are routed to a paralegal queue with the flagged claims highlighted.

# WRONG: checking the whole paragraph as one unit
# A paragraph with 5 true sentences and 1 false one passes easily
def check_output_bad(output: str, sources: list[str]) -> bool:
    score = nli_model.predict([(" ".join(sources), output)])[0][2]  # entailment
    return score > 0.7  # the 1 false sentence is averaged out

# RIGHT: split into atomic claims first, flag any ungrounded claim
def check_output_good(output: str, sources: list[str]) -> bool:
    claims = extract_claims(output)  # sentence-level or LLM-assisted splitting
    verifications = verify_claims(claims, sources)
    return all(v.is_grounded for v in verifications)  # ALL must pass
Using the generating model to verify itself

# WRONG: asking the same model instance to check its own output
# The model is biased toward confirming its own claims
def verify_bad(output: str) -> bool:
    check = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-opus-4-5",
        messages=[{"role": "user",
                   "content": f"Is the following factually correct? {output}"}]
    )
    return "yes" in check.content[0].text.lower()

# RIGHT: use an independent NLI classifier, not the generating model
# If you must use an LLM, use a separate model instance with a structured
# evaluation prompt that presents source chunks the generator did not see
def verify_good(output: str, sources: list[str]) -> bool:
    grounded, _ = is_output_grounded(output, sources)  # NLI-based, independent
    return grounded

Returning an empty string or a generic error when hallucinations are detected leaves users confused with no path forward. Always route flagged outputs to a defined resolution: abstain with an explanation, re-generate, or escalate to a human. Log each event with the flagged claims and resolution action -- this data is essential for threshold tuning and audit trails.
Always decompose output into atomic claims before running NLI. One ungrounded claim in a paragraph should fail the entire claim, not be averaged out.
The NLI classifier or verification LLM must be independent from the generating model. The same model that hallucinated cannot reliably detect its own fabrications.
Build a labeled evaluation set of grounded and hallucinated claim-source pairs. Sweep threshold values and pick the operating point that meets your precision-recall requirement.
Decide in advance what happens on hallucination detection: abstain, re-generate, or escalate. Do not implement silent failure.
When regenerating after a hallucination, widen the retrieval query. The original generation may have hallucinated because the relevant source chunk was not retrieved.
General NLI models underperform on specialized domains (clinical, legal, code). Fine-tune on domain-specific entailment pairs if hallucination rates remain high after threshold tuning.
Monitor hallucination rate as a system health metric:
A sustained increase signals model drift, retrieval quality degradation, or a new query distribution that the system is not handling well.

When a claim cannot be verified, tell users that the information is uncertain rather than suppressing the answer entirely. 'Based on available sources, but could not be fully verified' is more useful than a blank response.

Hallucination verification adds latency proportional to the number of claims extracted and the number of source chunks compared. For an output with 8 claims and 10 retrieved chunks, the NLI model runs 80 inference calls. Batching and asynchronous execution are essential at scale.
Batch all claims in one forward pass

Cross-encoder NLI models are efficient when all (source, claim) pairs for a single response are batched into a single forward pass. For 8 claims and 10 source chunks, this is a batch of 80 pairs -- most cross-encoders handle this in a single GPU forward pass in under 50ms total, versus sequential calls that would take 800ms.

Provides cross-encoder NLI models such as cross-encoder/nli-deberta-v3-base and cross-encoder/nli-MiniLM2-L6-H768. The latter is 4x smaller with only minor accuracy loss -- useful for latency-sensitive pipelines.
Open-source RAG evaluation framework that computes faithfulness scores (claim-level NLI against retrieved context) and answer relevance. Useful for offline evaluation as well as online guardrailing.
Evaluation and monitoring framework for LLM applications. Provides groundedness, context relevance, and answer relevance metrics with logging and dashboard support.
Output scanner suite that includes a FactualConsistency scanner using NLI and a NoRefusal scanner. Integrates as middleware in FastAPI pipelines.
Framework for specifying output validators including RAIL specs that can enforce grounding requirements via pluggable validators.

# RAGAS faithfulness evaluation (offline evaluation / batch guardrail)
from datasets import Dataset
from ragas import evaluate
from ragas.metrics import faithfulness

# Each row: question, contexts (retrieved), answer (generated)
data = {
    "question": ["When was the Eiffel Tower built?"],
    "contexts": [[
        "The Eiffel Tower was built between 1887 and 1889.",
        "Gustave Eiffel designed it as the entrance arch for the 1889 World's Fair."
    ]],
    "answer": ["The Eiffel Tower was built in 1887 and completed by 1889."],
}
dataset = Dataset.from_dict(data)
result = evaluate(dataset, metrics=[faithfulness])
print(result)  # {'faithfulness': 1.0} -- all claims grounded

Think of a hallucination guardrail as a fact-checker standing between the journalist (the LLM) and the printing press (the user or downstream system). The journalist writes fluently and quickly but sometimes invents sources under deadline pressure. The fact-checker does not rewrite the article -- they verify each claim against primary sources and send unverifiable claims back to the journalist for correction before anything goes to print.

The key insight is that the fact-checker's job is bounded: they only check claims against sources they have access to. Claims outside their source library get flagged as 'unverifiable', not 'false'. This is the right behavior -- admitting the limits of verification is preferable to either blindly passing everything or incorrectly failing legitimate content.
Understanding which type of hallucination you are targeting shapes which guardrail technique is appropriate. The main categories are:

The generated output contradicts the source documents provided in the prompt. This is the most detectable type -- NLI entailment against retrieved chunks catches it reliably. Example: a summary that states a document 'recommended increasing budget by 30%' when the document stated 'recommended a 15% reduction'.

The output adds information that is neither supported nor contradicted by the provided sources -- the model fills gaps from parametric memory. This is harder to detect because NLI cannot distinguish 'information not in sources' from 'information contradicting sources' unless you explicitly check that every claim has a supporting source (not just that no claim contradicts one).

The model generates content from training data memory that is outdated, incorrect, or never existed -- invented APIs, wrong dates, fabricated citations. Self-consistency sampling helps here but does not eliminate the problem if the error is consistent across all training samples. External knowledge bases or structured tool calls (fetching live data) are the most effective mitigation.

In agentic systems, hallucinations manifest differently than in conversational settings. The model generates structured outputs (tool calls, JSON payloads, code) where a fabrication is not a wrong sentence but a wrong action.
The agent invents a parameter value not present in the conversation or context (e.g., fabricates a user ID, account number, or file path).
Mitigation: validate all tool arguments against the set of values mentioned in the conversation context before executing the tool call.
The agent generates a JSON field or function signature that does not exist in the actual API schema.
Mitigation: validate tool call JSON against the declared tool schema using strict JSON Schema validation before execution.
In multi-step plans, the agent references the result of a step that has not yet been executed, or misremembers the output of a previous step.
Mitigation: maintain a structured step log and cross-reference agent claims about previous steps against actual execution results before proceeding to the next step.

Attackers can craft inputs designed to cause the model to hallucinate specific false content -- for example, injecting a fabricated policy statement into a RAG corpus to make the model output incorrect procedures. Hallucination guardrails that verify against the retrieved corpus can catch this, but only if the malicious content is also in the retrieved chunks. Corpus integrity (ensuring the retrieval index has not been tampered with) is a prerequisite for trusted grounding.

NLI classifiers themselves can be fooled by carefully crafted text that semantically entails a false claim. For high-security applications, ensemble multiple NLI models from different architectures and flag outputs that show disagreement between classifiers. A claim that one model grades as entailed but another grades as contradiction warrants conservative handling (abstain or escalate).

NLI models degrade when query language differs from training distribution (e.g., technical jargon, code-mixed text, non-standard spelling). Monitor NLI score distributions over time and trigger review when the average drops.
LLM model upgrades can change hallucination rate and type. Re-evaluate the guardrail threshold after any model change using the same labeled evaluation set.

Retrieval quality affects grounding check reliability. If retrieval recall drops (the relevant document is not retrieved), the grounding check will flag correct claims that simply lack supporting context. Monitor retrieval recall alongside hallucination metrics.
Hallucination guardrails are output-side checks that verify factual grounding after generation, independent of the model's internal confidence signals.

NLI entailment against retrieved source chunks is the most effective technique for RAG systems -- verify at the atomic claim level, not the paragraph level, to avoid averaging out individual fabrications.
Self-consistency sampling detects model uncertainty without a corpus, but measures agreement among samples rather than ground truth -- a model can be consistently wrong.
In agentic systems, hallucinations become actions: validate tool arguments against conversation context and JSON schemas before execution, not after.
The verifier must be independent from the generator -- the same model that hallucinated cannot reliably detect its own fabrications.
Calibrate NLI thresholds empirically against a labeled domain-specific evaluation set; the right operating point trades false positives against false negatives based on application risk.
Define resolution actions (abstain, re-generate, escalate) before deployment and log all hallucination events to enable monitoring and threshold tuning over time.
